title: Expressing the support of the House of Representatives for the naming of new or undedicated facilities of the Department of Veterans Affairs after women veterans and minority veterans in order to reflect the diversity of all who have served in the Armed Forces of the United States.
plain_summary: This congressional resolution asks the House of Representatives to support naming new Veterans Affairs buildings after women and minority veterans. Right now, only about 20 out of more than 1,700 VA facilities are named after women or minority veterans. The resolution says this should change because women and minorities have served and sacrificed for the country for a long time. The goal is to make sure the names of VA buildings better represent all the different types of people who have served in the military.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Veterans' Affairs.

sections.title: Title and Introduction
sections.text: This is a resolution from the House of Representatives. It shows support for naming new Department of Veterans Affairs buildings after women veterans and minority veterans. The goal is to honor the diverse groups of people who have served in the United States military.

sections.title: Current Naming Statistics
sections.text: As of December two thousand twenty four, the Department of Veterans Affairs has over one thousand seven hundred health care buildings. Out of all these facilities, only sixteen are named after people from minority groups and only four are named after women veterans.

sections.title: Historical Service of Women and Minorities
sections.text: Millions of women and people from minority groups have served in the United States military since the country was founded. During the Revolutionary War, Margaret Corbin and Deborah Sampson were among the first women to fight in combat. Other women served as spies, including one known as Agent three fifty five in George Washington's spy network. During the Civil War, hundreds of women disguised themselves as men to join the military, while others worked as nurses, spies, and administrators. Harriet Tubman became the first American woman known to lead troops in battle. Dr. Mary Edwards Walker was the first and only woman to receive the Medal of Honor for her Civil War service. Women became an official part of the military when the Army Nurse Corps started in nineteen oh one and the Navy Nurse Corps began in nineteen oh eight.

sections.title: Discrimination and Unrecognized Service
sections.text: Many of the nation's heroes were denied awards and recognition for their service because of systematic racial discrimination and anti-Semitism (hatred against Jewish people). Crispus Attucks was the first American killed in the Boston Massacre, which helped start the fight for American independence and freedom.

sections.title: Black Servicemembers' Contributions
sections.text: Black servicemembers have volunteered to fight in every major war since the Revolutionary War. They did this despite facing slavery, segregated military units, Jim Crow laws (laws that enforced racial separation), discriminatory voting laws, job restrictions, and being denied benefits for their service. The Tuskegee Airmen were the first African American pilots in the United States Air Force. During World War II, they destroyed over two hundred fifty German aircraft and over nine hundred fifty supply vehicles. These airmen received eight Purple Hearts, fifteen Bronze Stars, one hundred fifty Distinguished Flying Crosses, and nearly seven hundred fifty other Air Medals.

sections.title: LGBTQ Service and Restrictions
sections.text: Members of the LGBTQ community (lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, and queer people) and their supporters fought for the right to serve openly in the military and eventually succeeded in lifting those restrictions. However, recent administrative actions have created new restrictions that limit the ability of transgender people to serve openly. Harvey Milk, one of the first openly gay elected officials in the United States, previously served in the United States Navy as a diving officer.

sections.title: Native American Service
sections.text: American Indians and Alaska Natives serve in the military at the highest rate per person compared to any other population group. This includes the Cherokee, Choctaw, and Assiniboine code talkers of World War I and the Comanche, Mohawk, Muscogee, and Navajo code talkers of World War II. Code talkers used their native languages to send secret military messages.

sections.title: Latino and Asian American Service
sections.text: Sixty Latino servicemembers have received the Medal of Honor, including eight from Puerto Rico and seven who were born outside the United States. One of them was Rodolfo P. Hernandez, the son of farmworkers who earned the Medal of Honor for his brave actions and near-death injuries in Korea. Asian American, Native Hawaiian, and Pacific Islander people have served in the United States military since the War of eighteen twelve. This includes Telesforo Trinidad, the only member of the military to receive a Medal of Honor for actions during peacetime.

sections.title: Diverse Military Roles and Appreciation
sections.text: Women and members of minority groups have served in every type of military job, from infantry soldier to cyber warfare engineer. The United States is proud of and appreciates the service of all women veterans and minority veterans who have shown great courage and commitment to defending the principles the United States was founded on and continues to support.

sections.title: Benefits of Naming Facilities
sections.text: By naming new or unnamed Department of Veterans Affairs facilities after women veterans and minority veterans, the House of Representatives could highlight the historic and continuing presence of women and minority groups in the Armed Forces and National Guard. It would also pay respect to women veterans and minority veterans for their brave service and sacrifice for the United States.

sections.title: Resolution
sections.text: Therefore, the House of Representatives supports naming new or unnamed Department of Veterans Affairs facilities after women veterans and minority veterans. This would reflect the diversity of all people who have served in the United States Armed Forces.
